title: Evaluating Prompt Stability
concept: Prompt templates must be evaluated across diverse test sets to ensure accuracy is robust to minor user wording variations.

options.text: A template that fills in user inputs to create a final prompt.
A hardcoded prompt that never changes.
A system to delete bad user messages.

exercises.explain: Prompt templates take user input and place it into a larger instruction set. We need to make sure the template works no matter what the user inputs.

cards.title: The Fragile AI
cards.body: Have you ever asked two friends the same question and gotten completely different answers? AI can do the same thing! If a user changes just one word in their message, a weak prompt template might completely fail. Let's learn how to make our AI robust!

cards.title: What is Prompt Stability?
cards.body: Prompt stability means that your prompt template gives accurate and consistent results, even when users write their inputs in slightly different ways. To find out if a prompt is stable, we test it against a large list of diverse test cases.

cards.title: The Umbrella Test
cards.body: Imagine testing a new umbrella. If you only test it in a light sprinkle, you don't know if it will break in a windstorm. Testing your prompt template is the same! You need to blast it with all kinds of user inputs—polite, messy, short, and long—to make sure it doesn't break.

cards.title: Translating Real Users
cards.body: Suppose your prompt template translates text into French. If you test it with 'Hello', 'Hi there!', and 'Good morning, how are you?', a stable prompt template will translate all of them correctly, without getting confused by the extra words.

cards.title: The 'One-Test' Myth
cards.body: Many people think that if a prompt works perfectly once, it is ready for production. In reality, a prompt that works for one specific input might fail catastrophically on the very next one. Continuous testing across diverse examples is key!

cards.title: Stable vs. Unstable
cards.body: Let's look at how a stable template handles different user styles compared to an unstable one.

right.text: Handles formal greetings, slang, and typos smoothly, always delivering the correct response format.

left.text: Works perfectly for 'Hello', but crashes or gives bad output when the user says 'Hey ya!' or 'Greetings, friend'.

exercises.prompt: Why should we evaluate prompt templates using a diverse test set?

options.text: To make the AI run faster.
To ensure the AI stays accurate despite variations in user wording.
To teach the AI how to write its own prompts.
To reduce the cost of running the AI model.

exercises.explain: Testing with many different wording styles ensures the template doesn't break when real users type in their unique ways.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A prompt template is considered stable if it only works when the user types a single, specific phrase.
exercises.explain: If a prompt template only works for one specific phrasing, it will fail when real users type their inputs differently.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the rule for testing prompt templates.
exercises.sentence: To make sure a prompt template is stable, we must test it against a set of inputs that are highly [blank].
exercises.explain: A diverse test set contains many different styles of writing, ensuring the prompt works for everyone.

exercises.prompt: A prompt template is supposed to extract vehicle issues into JSON format. Which of the following shows an UNSTABLE prompt template?

options.text: Input: 'Check engine light' -> Output: {'issue': 'engine'}. Input: 'Please check engine light' -> Output: {'issue': 'engine'}.
Input: 'Check engine light' -> Output: {'issue': 'engine'}. Input: 'Please check engine light' -> Output: 'I cannot help with polite requests.'
Input: 'Check engine light' -> Output: {'issue': 'engine'}. Input: 'Fix my engine' -> Output: {'issue': 'engine'}.

exercises.explain: Option B is generated by a AI helper because it behaves inconsistently (gives the wrong format) just because the user added a polite word. A stable prompt should always output JSON.

exercises.prompt: You are building a customer support chatbot that uses a prompt template. What is the best way to make sure it handles all kinds of customer messages successfully?

options.text: Only write short prompts because users never write long ones.
Test the prompt template using a diverse set of user inputs.
Manually rewrite the prompt template every time a user types something new.
Hope that all users type exactly the same way.

exercises.explain: Evaluating prompt stability across a diverse test set is the only way to ensure your AI application remains reliable and accurate when real users start typing their unique prompts.
